items.title: Security requirements are defined and tracked
items.description: Functional and non-functional security requirements (authN/authZ, data classification, regulatory obligations) are captured as first-class backlog items, not assumed.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether security/abuse-case requirements are captured as tracked work items (not just functional stories):

```
# review a sample of epics/stories for security acceptance criteria
```
items.how_to_fix: Define security requirements (auth, data protection, abuse cases) per feature, track them as first-class backlog items with acceptance criteria, and verify closure. Re-audit a sample of stories.

items.title: Threat modeling is performed during design
items.description: New features and architectural changes undergo structured threat modeling (e.g., STRIDE) to identify trust boundaries, attack surfaces, and mitigations before code is written.
items.how_to_find: Check whether high-risk designs have a threat model (DFD + STRIDE) before build.
items.how_to_fix: Make threat modeling a design-phase gate for high-risk changes, capture threats + mitigations as tracked items, and keep models updated. Re-verify recent designs were modeled.

items.title: Security gates block insecure promotions
items.description: CI/CD pipelines enforce policy gates (failing builds on critical findings, required reviews, signed artifacts) that prevent code from advancing without meeting a security bar.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether pipeline security gates (SAST/SCA/secrets/IaC) actually fail the build vs warn-only:

```
grep -REn 'continue-on-error|allow_failure|soft_fail' .github/workflows/ .gitlab-ci.yml
```

`continue-on-error: true` on security steps = gate is advisory only.
items.how_to_fix: Configure security checks to fail the pipeline on policy-violating findings (no `continue-on-error`), with a documented risk-accept exception path. Re-verify gates block promotion.

items.title: Security design reviews are mandatory for high-risk changes
items.description: High-blast-radius changes (auth, crypto, payment, multi-tenant boundaries) require a documented security design review before implementation proceeds.
items.how_to_find: Check whether high-risk changes require a security review sign-off (via CODEOWNERS / process).
items.how_to_fix: Require security review for high-risk changes (new auth, crypto, external exposure, PII), enforce via CODEOWNERS + policy, and track sign-off. Re-audit recent high-risk PRs.

sections.title: Secure SDLC Integration — Developer Enablement & Training

items.title: Secure coding training is delivered and tracked
items.description: Developers receive role-relevant secure coding training (OWASP Top 10, language-specific pitfalls) with completion tracked and refreshed periodically.
items.how_to_find: Check for recorded secure-coding training completion per developer.
items.how_to_fix: Deliver role-based secure-coding training with tracked completion + refreshers, and tie topics to observed defect classes. Re-verify completion rates.

items.title: A security champions program exists
items.description: Each delivery team has an embedded security champion who reviews designs, triages findings, and acts as the local liaison to the central security team.
items.how_to_find: Check whether teams have designated security champions with defined responsibilities.
items.how_to_fix: Establish a security champions program (a champion per team, regular sync, clear duties), and empower them in reviews/threat modeling. Re-verify coverage across teams.

items.title: Secure coding standards and golden paths are published
items.description: The organization publishes secure coding standards, hardened libraries, and paved-road templates so developers default to safe patterns.
items.how_to_find: Check for published, discoverable secure-coding standards and paved-road libraries/templates.
items.how_to_fix: Publish secure coding standards + golden-path templates/libraries (auth, crypto, input handling) that are easy to adopt, and keep them current. Re-verify they exist and are used.

items.title: Security findings drive targeted education
items.description: Recurring vulnerability classes from scanning and pentests feed back into training content and guidance, closing the knowledge gap that produced them.
items.how_to_find: Check whether recurring finding types feed back into targeted training/guidance.
items.how_to_fix: Analyze finding trends and target education/guardrails at the top defect classes, closing the loop between detection and prevention. Re-verify feedback loop.

sections.title: Automated Security Testing — Static Application Security Testing (SAST)

items.title: SAST runs automatically in the pipeline
items.description: Static analysis executes on every pull request and merge, scanning all in-scope repositories rather than running only ad hoc.
items.how_to_find:
Check that SAST runs on every PR/merge:

```
grep -REn 'semgrep|codeql|sonar|bandit|gosec|brakeman' .github/workflows/ .gitlab-ci.yml
```
items.how_to_fix: Integrate SAST into CI on every PR, fail on high-severity findings, and keep scan times fast (incremental). Re-verify SAST runs automatically.

items.title: A SAST baseline separates new from legacy findings
items.description: A committed baseline allows the pipeline to fail only on newly introduced issues, avoiding alert fatigue from a legacy backlog while still tracking it.
items.how_to_find: Check whether SAST distinguishes newly introduced findings from pre-existing debt (baseline/diff mode).
items.how_to_fix: Establish a baseline so PRs are gated on new findings while legacy debt is tracked/burned down separately. Re-verify diff-aware gating.

items.title: False positives are managed with auditable suppressions
items.description: Suppressions and rule tuning are version-controlled, justified, and reviewed, so the tool stays trustworthy and suppression is not abused to hide real risk.
items.how_to_find:
Check how suppressions are recorded:

```
grep -REn 'nosec|nosemgrep|codeql\[|SuppressWarnings' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Require justification + reviewer approval for suppressions, keep them in-code/auditable, expire/re-review them, and prefer fixing. Re-audit suppressions.

items.title: SAST ruleset coverage matches the tech stack
items.description: Enabled rulesets cover every primary language and framework in use, including security-relevant categories like injection, deserialization, and SSRF.
items.how_to_find: Check whether SAST rules cover all languages/frameworks in use.
items.how_to_fix: Enable language/framework-appropriate rulesets (+ custom rules for internal sinks), review coverage vs the stack, and update as it changes. Re-verify coverage.

sections.title: Automated Security Testing — Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST)

items.title: DAST scans run automatically against deployed builds
items.description: Dynamic scans execute against a staging/test deployment as part of the pipeline, catching runtime issues SAST cannot see.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether DAST runs against a deployed environment in the pipeline:

```
grep -REn 'zap|dast|nuclei|burp' .github/workflows/ .gitlab-ci.yml
```
items.how_to_fix: Automate DAST against a deployed staging build in CI/CD (baseline on PR, full periodically), and triage results. Re-verify DAST runs.

items.title: Authenticated and API scanning is configured
items.description: DAST is configured with valid sessions and API specifications so it reaches authenticated surfaces and exercises real endpoints, not just public pages.
items.how_to_find: Check whether DAST authenticates and scans APIs (not just unauthenticated pages), using an OpenAPI spec where available.
items.how_to_fix: Configure authenticated DAST (session/token) + API scanning from the OpenAPI/GraphQL schema so protected functionality and APIs are covered. Re-verify auth + API coverage.

items.title: DAST runs against safe, non-production targets
items.description: Active scanning is pointed at dedicated test environments with seeded data to avoid disrupting production or corrupting live data.
items.how_to_find: Check that DAST targets staging/ephemeral envs, not production (to avoid impact).
items.how_to_fix: Run DAST against dedicated non-prod/ephemeral environments with representative data, and gate any prod scanning tightly. Re-verify target environments.

items.title: DAST findings are deduplicated and triaged
items.description: Results are correlated across scans to reduce noise, and confirmed issues are tracked to remediation with severity-based SLAs.
items.how_to_find: Check whether DAST results are deduplicated across runs and triaged into tickets.
items.how_to_fix: Deduplicate findings (fingerprinting), auto-create/track tickets, suppress confirmed false positives with justification, and monitor trends. Re-verify triage flow.

sections.title: Automated Security Testing — Software Composition Analysis (SCA)

items.title: Dependencies are scanned for known vulnerabilities
items.description: Every build scans direct and transitive dependencies against vulnerability databases and fails or flags on critical CVEs.
items.how_to_find:
Check for SCA in the pipeline:

```
grep -REn 'osv-scanner|trivy|snyk|dependency-check|npm audit|pip-audit' .github/workflows/
```
items.how_to_fix: Run SCA on every build, fail on high/critical (with exception path), and monitor continuously. Re-verify SCA runs.

references.title: OWASP Top Ten — Vulnerable and Outdated Components

items.title: License compliance is enforced
items.description: SCA evaluates dependency licenses against an allow/deny policy to prevent introduction of incompatible or restrictive licenses.
items.how_to_find: Check whether dependency licenses are scanned against an allowed/denied policy.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce a license policy in CI (allowlist/denylist), flag non-compliant licenses, and track exceptions. Re-verify license gating.

items.title: SCA prioritizes reachable, exploitable vulnerabilities
items.description: Findings are prioritized by exploitability (reachability, EPSS, KEV) so teams remediate truly risky issues first rather than chasing every CVE.
items.how_to_find: Check whether SCA prioritizes by reachability/exploitability (not just CVSS) to reduce noise.
items.how_to_fix: Use reachability analysis + EPSS/KEV context to prioritize truly exploitable vulns, while still tracking the rest. Re-verify prioritization.

references.title: OWASP Top Ten — Vulnerable and Outdated Components

items.title: Automated dependency updates are enabled
items.description: Automated update tooling proposes upgrades for vulnerable and outdated dependencies, keeping the dependency surface current with minimal toil.
items.how_to_find:
Check for automated update tooling:

```
ls .github/dependabot.yml renovate.json 2>/dev/null
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable Dependabot/Renovate with grouped, tested auto-updates and prompt review/merge, prioritizing security updates. Re-verify automation.

items.title: Pre-commit secret scanning is in place
items.description: Developer machines run pre-commit hooks that block credentials, keys, and tokens before they ever reach the remote repository.
items.how_to_find:
Check for pre-commit secret hooks:

```
cat .pre-commit-config.yaml 2>/dev/null | grep -iE 'gitleaks|detect-secrets|trufflehog'
```
items.how_to_fix: Add pre-commit secret scanning (gitleaks/detect-secrets) so secrets are caught before commit, and make it easy to install. Re-verify the hook.

items.title: Pipeline secret scanning enforces a backstop
items.description: CI scans every push and PR for secrets as a server-side backstop that does not depend on developers enabling local hooks.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether CI scans for secrets even if pre-commit is bypassed:

```
grep -REn 'gitleaks|trufflehog|detect-secrets' .github/workflows/ .gitlab-ci.yml
```
items.how_to_fix: Run secret scanning in CI + enable platform push protection as a backstop, failing on detections. Re-verify pipeline scanning.

items.title: Repository history is scanned for leaked secrets
items.description: Full git history is periodically scanned because secrets removed from HEAD may still be retrievable in prior commits.
items.how_to_find:
Scan full history (secrets removed from HEAD persist in git history):

```
gitleaks detect --source . --log-opts="--all"
```
items.how_to_fix: Scan history, rotate any secret ever committed, and purge from history (filter-repo/BFG) where required. Re-scan history.

items.title: Detected secrets trigger immediate rotation
items.description: A defined process treats any exposed secret as compromised: revoke, rotate, and review for abuse, regardless of repository visibility.
items.how_to_find: Check whether there's an automated/defined process to rotate a leaked secret fast.
items.how_to_fix: Treat any detected secret as compromised: automate/runbook immediate rotation + revocation, and verify the old value no longer works. Re-verify the rotation process.

items.title: Secrets are stored in a centralized secrets manager
items.description: Application and infrastructure secrets live in a dedicated secrets manager with access control and audit logging, not in source, env files, or wikis.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether apps pull secrets from a manager (Vault/cloud secrets) vs env/config files:

```
grep -REn 'vault|secretsmanager|SecretManager|KeyVault|getSecret' src/ config/
```
items.how_to_fix: Store all secrets in a centralized manager with access control + audit, inject at runtime, and remove secrets from code/config. Re-verify usage.

items.title: Dynamic, short-lived secrets are used where possible
items.description: Databases, cloud APIs, and services receive dynamically generated, short-TTL credentials instead of long-lived static keys.
items.how_to_find: Check whether DB/cloud creds are dynamic/short-lived vs static.
items.how_to_fix: Use dynamic secrets (e.g. Vault DB engine, cloud STS) with short TTLs where supported, reducing standing credentials. Re-verify dynamic secrets.

items.title: Static secrets are rotated automatically
items.description: Any unavoidable long-lived secrets are rotated on a defined schedule and on suspected compromise, with rotation automated to avoid drift.
items.how_to_find: Check whether remaining static secrets have automated rotation + expiry.
items.how_to_fix: Automate rotation of static secrets on a schedule (and on leak), track age, and alert on stale secrets. Re-audit rotation.

items.title: Workloads authenticate via identity, not embedded keys
items.description: Services retrieve secrets using platform workload identity (e.g., cloud IAM roles, Kubernetes service accounts) rather than bootstrapping with embedded credentials.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether workloads use workload identity (IRSA/managed identity/SPIFFE) vs embedded keys:

```
grep -REn 'AWS_ACCESS_KEY|service-account|workload-identity|azure.*identity' src/ k8s/
```
items.how_to_fix: Adopt workload identity federation (IRSA, GKE/Azure workload identity, SPIFFE) so workloads get short-lived creds without embedded keys. Re-verify identity-based auth.

items.title: Pipeline secrets are masked and stored securely
items.description: CI secrets are stored as protected/masked variables or pulled from the secrets manager at runtime, never committed to the repository.
items.how_to_find: Check that CI secrets are stored as encrypted/masked secrets, not plain variables.
items.how_to_fix: Use the CI encrypted secret store (masked + protected), never plain variables, and scope to protected branches/environments. Re-verify secret storage.

items.title: Secrets never appear in build logs or artifacts
items.description: Logging is configured to redact secrets and pipelines avoid echoing sensitive values; artifacts are inspected to ensure no credentials are baked in.
items.how_to_find:
Check logs/artifacts for leaked secrets:

```
grep -REn 'set -x|echo .*secret|printenv' .github/workflows/
trivy image --scanners secret <image>
```
items.how_to_fix: Rely on masking, avoid `set -x`/echoing secrets, keep secrets out of artifacts (multi-stage builds), and rotate leaks. Re-verify logs/artifacts.

items.title: Pipeline secrets are scoped and least-privilege
items.description: Secrets are scoped to specific environments, branches, and jobs so a compromised pipeline cannot reach production credentials it does not need.
items.how_to_find: Check whether pipeline secrets/tokens are scoped to the minimum repos/environments/permissions.
items.how_to_fix: Scope secrets to specific repos/environments, least-privilege token permissions, and avoid org-wide sensitive secrets. Re-audit scope.

items.title: Cloud access uses OIDC federation, not static keys
items.description: Pipelines authenticate to cloud providers via short-lived OIDC-federated tokens rather than long-lived static cloud access keys stored as CI secrets.
items.how_to_find:
Check for static cloud keys vs OIDC in pipelines:

```
grep -REn 'AWS_ACCESS_KEY|id-token:|permissions:.*id-token|aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials' .github/workflows/
```
items.how_to_fix: Use OIDC federation to assume short-lived cloud roles (constrained trust to repo/branch), removing static keys. Re-verify OIDC usage.

items.title: Dependencies are pinned with committed lockfiles
items.description: Lockfiles pin exact versions and integrity hashes for all dependencies so builds are reproducible and tampering is detectable.
items.how_to_find:
Check for committed lockfiles + pinned versions:

```
ls package-lock.json yarn.lock poetry.lock go.sum Cargo.lock 2>/dev/null
grep -REn '\^|~|latest' package.json
```
items.how_to_fix: Commit lockfiles, pin versions, and install with `--frozen`/`npm ci` (integrity-verified, reproducible). Re-verify pinning.

references.title: SLSA — Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts
OWASP Top Ten — Software and Data Integrity Failures

items.title: Dependency confusion is mitigated
items.description: Private package scopes and registry configuration prevent attackers from publishing a malicious public package that shadows an internal name.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether internal package names could resolve from public registries:

```
grep -REn 'registry|index-url|scope|@<org>/' .npmrc pip.conf package.json
```
items.how_to_fix: Reserve internal names publicly, use scoped packages + a private proxy with controlled resolution order, and verify sources. Re-review registry config.

references.title: CNCF Software Supply Chain Best Practices

items.title: Dependencies are pulled through a trusted proxy
items.description: An internal artifact proxy/registry caches and vets external packages, providing a control point for blocking and integrity verification.
items.how_to_find: Check whether builds fetch packages via an internal proxy/mirror vs directly from public registries.
items.how_to_fix: Route dependency fetches through a trusted proxy/mirror (Artifactory/Nexus) with allowlisting + caching, so builds don't pull directly from public registries. Re-verify proxy usage.

references.title: CNCF Software Supply Chain Best Practices
SLSA — Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts

items.title: Dependency integrity is verified at install time
items.description: Package manager integrity checks (hash/signature verification) are enabled so corrupted or tampered packages fail the build.
items.how_to_find: Check whether install verifies integrity hashes/signatures (lockfile hashes, `--require-hashes`).
items.how_to_fix: Enforce integrity verification at install (lockfile hashes, `pip --require-hashes`, `npm ci`), and fail on mismatch. Re-verify integrity checks.

references.title: SLSA — Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts
OWASP Top Ten — Software and Data Integrity Failures

sections.title: Supply Chain Security — Artifact Signing & Provenance

items.title: Build artifacts are cryptographically signed
items.description: Containers and release artifacts are signed so consumers can verify authenticity and detect tampering before deployment.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether artifacts/images are signed in the pipeline:

```
cosign verify <image>
```
items.how_to_fix: Sign artifacts/images (cosign/Sigstore, keyless where possible) as part of the build. Re-verify signing.

references.title: SLSA — Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts

items.title: SBOMs are generated and published for releases
items.description: A software bill of materials is produced for each artifact, enabling rapid impact analysis when a new dependency CVE is disclosed.
items.how_to_find:
Check for SBOM generation:

```
syft <image> -o spdx-json ; # or trivy sbom
```
items.how_to_fix: Generate + publish an SBOM (SPDX/CycloneDX) per release, attach it to artifacts, and use it for continuous vuln monitoring. Re-verify SBOMs exist.

items.title: Build provenance attestations are produced
items.description: The pipeline emits SLSA provenance describing how, where, and from what sources an artifact was built, enabling verification of build integrity.
items.how_to_find:
Check for SLSA provenance attestations:

```
cosign verify-attestation --type slsaprovenance <image>
```
items.how_to_fix: Produce SLSA provenance in the build (what was built, from which source/commit, by which pipeline), and store attestations. Re-verify attestations.

items.title: Signature and provenance are verified before deploy
items.description: Admission controls reject unsigned artifacts or those without valid provenance, closing the loop between signing and enforcement.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether deploy/admission verifies signatures + provenance:

```
# k8s: policy-controller / Kyverno verifyImages ; check enforcement mode
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce signature + provenance verification at deploy/admission (fail closed on unsigned/unverified), pinned to trusted signers. Re-test that unsigned artifacts are rejected.

references.title: SLSA — Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts

sections.title: Supply Chain Security — Base Image Security

items.title: Base images come from trusted, pinned sources
items.description: Base images are sourced from a vetted internal registry or trusted publisher and pinned by digest, not floating tags like latest.
items.how_to_find:
Check base image sources + pinning:

```
grep -REn '^FROM ' **/Dockerfile   # trusted registry? pinned by digest?
```

`FROM image:latest` (mutable, untrusted) = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Use trusted/official base images pinned by digest (`@sha256:`), from an approved registry/mirror. Re-review Dockerfiles.

items.title: Minimal/distroless base images are used
items.description: Images are kept minimal (distroless or slim) to reduce attack surface, remove shells and package managers, and shrink the vulnerability footprint.
items.how_to_find:
Check image size/contents (full OS vs minimal/distroless):

```
grep -REn '^FROM ' **/Dockerfile
dive <image>   # inspect layers/contents
```
items.how_to_fix: Use minimal/distroless/alpine bases to shrink attack surface, remove build tools/shells from runtime images (multi-stage). Re-review base images.

items.title: Container images are scanned before and after publish
items.description: Images are scanned for OS and library vulnerabilities at build time and continuously in the registry as new CVEs are disclosed.
items.how_to_find:
Check for image scanning in CI + registry:

```
grep -REn 'trivy|grype|clair|snyk' .github/workflows/
```
items.how_to_fix: Scan images in CI (fail on high/critical) and continuously in the registry (new CVEs on existing images), and remediate. Re-verify scanning.

items.title: Base images are rebuilt and patched regularly
items.description: A defined cadence rebuilds images on updated bases so OS-level patches reach running workloads instead of lingering in stale layers.
items.how_to_find: Check how fresh deployed base images are (age/patch level).
items.how_to_fix: Rebuild images regularly from updated bases (automated), redeploy to pick up OS patches, and monitor base-image CVEs. Re-verify image freshness.

sections.title: Infrastructure as Code — IaC Misconfiguration Scanning

items.title: IaC is scanned for misconfigurations in CI
items.description: Terraform, CloudFormation, and Kubernetes manifests are scanned on every change for insecure defaults before infrastructure is provisioned.
items.how_to_find:
Check for IaC scanning:

```
grep -REn 'checkov|tfsec|trivy config|terrascan|kics' .github/workflows/
```
items.how_to_fix: Scan Terraform/CloudFormation/etc. in CI (Checkov/tfsec/trivy), fail on high-severity misconfigs, and track findings. Re-verify IaC scanning.

items.title: Kubernetes manifests are checked against hardening guidance
items.description: K8s manifests and Helm charts are scanned for insecure settings (privileged containers, host mounts, missing limits) against CIS Kubernetes guidance.
items.how_to_find:
Check for manifest/K8s scanning:

```
grep -REn 'kube-linter|kubesec|checkov|conftest|polaris' .github/workflows/
```
items.how_to_fix: Scan manifests/Helm against hardening guidance (kube-linter/kubesec/Polaris) in CI, fail on violations (privileged, no limits, hostPath), and remediate. Re-verify.

items.title: IaC is validated against cloud security baselines
items.description: Scanning rules map to cloud provider security baselines (encryption at rest, public exposure, logging) so provisioned resources meet a known bar.
items.how_to_find: Check whether IaC is validated against org cloud baselines (CIS/benchmarks) before apply.
items.how_to_fix: Validate IaC against cloud security baselines (custom policies mapping to CIS/org standards) in CI, and block non-compliant changes. Re-verify baseline checks.

items.title: IaC scan results are tracked and not bypassed
items.description: Findings flow into tracking with owners, and suppressions are explicit and auditable rather than silently disabling rules.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether IaC scan gates can be silently bypassed:

```
grep -REn 'soft_fail|continue-on-error|skip-check' .github/workflows/ *.tf
```
items.how_to_fix: Make IaC gates blocking, require justified exceptions for skips, track findings to closure, and audit suppressions. Re-verify gating.

sections.title: Infrastructure as Code — Policy as Code

items.title: Security guardrails are codified as policy
items.description: Organizational security policies (no public buckets, required tags, mandatory encryption) are expressed as code and evaluated automatically.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether guardrails exist as code (OPA/Rego, Kyverno, Sentinel) vs manual review:

```
grep -REn 'rego|Kyverno|ConstraintTemplate|sentinel' . 2>/dev/null
```
items.how_to_fix: Codify security guardrails as policy (OPA/Kyverno/Sentinel), version + review them, and apply consistently across pipelines/clusters. Re-verify policies exist.

items.title: Admission control enforces policy at deploy time
items.description: A policy engine in the cluster (or cloud) rejects non-compliant resources at admission, providing a runtime backstop beyond pipeline checks.
items.how_to_find:
Check for a K8s admission controller enforcing policy:

```
kubectl get validatingwebhookconfigurations,mutatingwebhookconfigurations
kubectl get constrainttemplates 2>/dev/null
```
items.how_to_fix: Deploy an admission controller (OPA Gatekeeper/Kyverno) in enforce mode to block non-compliant workloads (privileged, unsigned images, no limits). Re-test enforcement.

items.title: Policies are unit-tested and reviewed
items.description: Policy code has its own tests and change review so guardrails behave as intended and cannot be weakened without scrutiny.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether policies have tests + go through review:

```
# opa test ./policy/ ; kyverno test .
```
items.how_to_fix: Unit-test policies (opa test / kyverno test), review changes like code, and validate before rollout to avoid gaps/outages. Re-verify tests exist.

items.title: Policy coverage spans pipeline and runtime
items.description: The same intent is enforced both in CI (fast feedback) and at runtime (authoritative), avoiding gaps where one layer is the only control.
items.how_to_find: Check whether policy is enforced both in CI (pre-deploy) and at runtime (admission), not just one.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce policy at both stages: shift-left in CI (conftest/IaC) and at runtime via admission, with consistent rules. Re-verify coverage across both.

items.title: Security-relevant events are logged centrally
items.description: Authentication, authorization, and administrative actions are logged to a central, tamper-resistant store with sufficient retention.
items.how_to_find: Check whether auth, access, admin, and deploy events are centrally logged.
items.how_to_fix: Log security-relevant events (authn/authz, admin actions, deploys, policy denials) to a central SIEM with retention + access control. Re-verify coverage.

references.title: OWASP Top Ten — Security Logging and Monitoring Failures

items.title: Actionable security alerts are configured
items.description: Detections for suspicious behavior (brute force, privilege escalation, anomalous egress) generate alerts routed to an owner with a response path.
items.how_to_find: Check whether alerts exist for key threats and are tuned (not noise) and routed to responders.
items.how_to_fix: Configure high-signal, actionable alerts for priority threats, route to on-call, tune to reduce noise, and document response. Re-verify alerting.

references.title: OWASP Top Ten — Security Logging and Monitoring Failures

items.title: Runtime threat detection is deployed for workloads
items.description: Runtime sensors detect anomalous container/host behavior (unexpected process execution, file tampering, suspicious syscalls) in production.
items.how_to_find:
Check for runtime detection (Falco/EDR/CWPP) on workloads:

```
kubectl get pods -n falco 2>/dev/null
```
items.how_to_fix: Deploy runtime threat detection (Falco/eBPF/CWPP/EDR) with a maintained ruleset, alerting on anomalous process/network/file activity. Re-verify detection is running.

items.title: Configuration drift is detected and reconciled
items.description: Running infrastructure is continuously compared to its declared IaC state so manual or unauthorized changes are surfaced and reconciled.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether infra drift from IaC is detected:

```
terraform plan   # non-empty diff on unchanged code = drift
```
items.how_to_fix: Detect drift (scheduled plan / GitOps reconciliation) and reconcile back to declared state, alerting on out-of-band changes. Re-verify drift detection.

sections.title: Runtime & Monitoring — Remediation SLAs & Feedback Loop

items.title: Vulnerability remediation SLAs are defined and tracked
items.description: Time-to-remediate targets are set by severity and tracked, so critical vulnerabilities are fixed within a bounded, measurable window.
items.how_to_find: Check whether remediation SLAs by severity exist and are measured (time-to-remediate).
items.how_to_fix: Define severity-based remediation SLAs, track them on a dashboard, escalate breaches, and report trends. Re-verify SLA tracking.

items.title: Production signals feed back into the pipeline
items.description: Incidents, runtime detections, and pentest findings generate new pipeline checks, threat-model updates, or guidance so the same class does not recur.
items.how_to_find: Check whether runtime/prod findings (incidents, WAF, runtime alerts) create backlog items feeding earlier stages.
items.how_to_fix: Close the loop: route production security signals into the backlog + guardrails/tests so recurring issues are prevented earlier. Re-verify the feedback loop.

items.title: A vulnerability disclosure intake exists
items.description: External researchers have a clear, monitored channel to report vulnerabilities, and reports are triaged into the same remediation workflow.
items.how_to_find:
Check for a public disclosure channel:

```
curl -s https://<domain>/.well-known/security.txt
```
items.how_to_fix: Publish a `security.txt` + disclosure/VDP policy, provide a monitored intake, and triage external reports on defined SLAs. Re-verify the intake.

items.title: Program metrics drive continuous improvement
items.description: Trend metrics (mean time to remediate, escape rate, coverage) are reviewed regularly to steer investment and demonstrate program maturity.
items.how_to_find: Check whether DevSecOps metrics (coverage, MTTR, escape rate) are tracked and reviewed.
items.how_to_fix: Track program metrics (tool coverage, MTTR, defect escape rate, training) and review regularly to prioritize improvements. Re-verify metrics + review cadence.
